current_fact.fact_text: On 10 March 1876, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful telephone call, summoning his assistant with the words, “Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you.” That crackling sentence, transmitted over a wire in Boston, opened an era in which human voices could leap across cities and, eventually, around the world.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

current_fact.match_reason: The birth of long-distance voice communication aligns with Sedna’s first broadcast, both marking the beginning of new ways to send voices into the world.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1873, audiences in the Caucasus watched something entirely new: the premiere of "The Adventures of the Vizier of the Khan of Lenkaran," the first Azerbaijani play. Written by Mirza Fatali Akhundov, it helped ignite a local theatrical tradition that blended satire, social critique, and Eastern storytelling on a modern stage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: The birth of a national theatrical tradition resonates with an episode tracing the evolution of flamenco as a staged, culturally rich art form.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1933, the Long Beach earthquake jolted Southern California with a magnitude of 6.4, toppling hundreds of school buildings and killing around 108 people. The devastation sparked sweeping changes: California rewrote its building codes, turning tragedy into one of the earliest large-scale experiments in designing cities to ride out earthquakes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A sudden seismic jolt is mirrored by an episode themed around electric shocks and high-energy riffs shaking you awake.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1909, the Anglo-Siamese Treaty redrew the map of Southeast Asia, with Siam (modern Thailand) ceding Kedah, Kelantan, Perlis, and Terengganu to become British protectorates in Malaya. The quiet strokes of diplomats’ pens would later shape borders, identities, and the patchwork of cultures across the Malay Peninsula.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A treaty that forever altered Thailand’s borders naturally pairs with an episode recorded in Thailand, reflecting on place, identity, and movement.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 298, the Roman emperor Maximian capped his North African campaign with a triumphal entry into Carthage, parading military success through streets once ruled by Rome's old rival. His celebration in the province of Africa showcased how Rome turned distant territories into stages for imperial spectacle and power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A Roman triumph in Carthage sits on the same Mediterranean shores that the episode sails along musically, linking ancient ports and modern coastal sounds.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1814, Napoleon’s fortunes dimmed at the Battle of Laon, where his exhausted army clashed with the numerically superior forces of the Sixth Coalition. Though a master of battlefield improvisation, he couldn't turn the tide here—Laon became one of the setbacks that nudged his empire toward collapse.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A turning-point defeat in an empire’s story fits an episode about weathering storms and accepting that even long rains eventually pass.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1944, amid the Axis occupation of Greece, resistance groups formed the Political Committee of National Liberation as a rival government in the mountains. It ran courts, schools, and local administration—an underground experiment in democracy born in the middle of war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A clandestine liberation committee parallels Tuareg music born of exile and resistance, both channeling political struggle into new forms of community.

queue.fact_text: On the night of 10 March 1945, American B-29 bombers dropped incendiaries over Tokyo in a raid that turned wooden neighborhoods into a firestorm. In just a few hours, more than 100,000 people—mostly civilians—were killed, making it one of the deadliest air attacks in human history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: An episode centered on politically charged music and the violence of modern warfare matches the devastating, contested legacy of Tokyo’s firebombing.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1949, American broadcaster Mildred Gillars—better known as "Axis Sally"—was convicted of treason for her Nazi propaganda broadcasts during World War II. Her case tested how far freedom of speech extends when a citizen's voice is used as a weapon against their own country.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A story about a voice used for propaganda is contrasted with an episode about a voice used for resistance and political critique, highlighting radio’s power in opposite directions.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1952, Fulgencio Batista seized power in Cuba through a military coup, suspending elections and setting the stage for years of authoritarian rule. His regime’s corruption and inequality would later fuel the Cuban Revolution that overthrew him in 1959.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A Caribbean coup that reshaped Cuban and regional politics pairs with an episode exploring a Caribbean-born genre that later became a powerful cultural and political voice.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1966, South Vietnam’s prime minister Nguyễn Cao Kỳ abruptly dismissed popular General Nguyễn Chánh Thi, triggering protests and a crisis that fractured both the army and society. The power struggle revealed how fragile the anti-communist government was, even before facing its enemies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A tale of male military power struggles is counterbalanced with an episode about women claiming space and power through rock, echoing shifts in who gets to lead and be heard.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1990, Haitian leader Prosper Avril was forced from power just eighteen months after taking control in a coup. His ouster continued a turbulent cycle of coups and counter-coups that made Haiti’s late 20th century politics feel like a storm with no eye.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A story of political instability and dislocation is paired with an episode about using movement and travel to find clarity and a different perspective on turbulence.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 2000, the NASDAQ Composite peaked at 5,048.62, the dizzying summit of the dot-com bubble. In those months, companies with no profits but catchy URLs were valued in the billions—until gravity returned and the market crashed, erasing trillions of dollars and reshaping the tech industry.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: The peak of an internet-fueled stock frenzy pairs with an episode dissecting our entanglement with digital tech and the psychological cost of always being connected.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 2006’s future echo—10 March 2023—Silicon Valley Bank collapsed after a sudden run on deposits, becoming the second-largest bank failure in U.S. history. It was a 21st-century panic: rumors spread online, customers moved billions with a few taps, and a niche tech lender turned into a global financial scare overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A dramatic financial failure invites the reflective, learning-focused lens of this episode—rethinking risk, mistakes, and what systemic ‘failure’ can teach.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 2017, South Korea’s Constitutional Court unanimously upheld the impeachment of President Park Geun-hye, removing her from office after a massive corruption scandal. Millions of citizens had protested for months in peaceful "candlelight rallies," showing how sustained public pressure could reset a democracy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: A landmark moment involving a female head of state and citizen-driven accountability pairs with an episode elevating powerful female voices challenging the status quo.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 1909’s echo into the digital age, another border quietly shifted: but back in 1909 itself, it was legal lines, not fiber optics, that changed. The Anglo-Siamese Treaty’s cartographic tweaks still shape where cultures meet—and sometimes clash—across Southeast Asia today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: This reflective take on how borders shape landscapes and identities mirrors an episode that weaves geography, memory, and music into a shared sense of place.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March in the Roman province of Africa, Maximian’s triumph in Carthage showed how empires turn geography into narrative. The same streets had once belonged to a rival civilization; by marching through them in victory, Rome rewrote the city’s story in its own language.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A Roman province in North Africa and a modern African musical revolution are linked: both show how the continent remains a stage for power, culture, and rhythm that travels far beyond its borders.

queue.fact_text: On 10 March 2017’s courtroom drama in Seoul and 10 March 1949’s trial in Washington, two very different women faced very public judgments. Where Park Geun-hye’s impeachment followed mass protest, Axis Sally’s case probed how far one voice could betray a country in wartime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: Contrasting harmful propaganda with empowering female artistry, this pairing highlights how women’s voices can harm or heal collective stories.

queue.fact_text: Across 10 Marchs, from earthquakes and coups to space probes and theatrical debuts, history reveals how one date can hold multitudes. It’s a reminder that on any given day, somewhere on Earth—or Mars, or in a quiet radio studio on Sedna—someone is starting something that might later feel historic.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: An overview reflection on the date itself fits an episode about journeys and destinations, inviting listeners to see this March 10 as part of a larger, ongoing voyage.

published.fact_text: On 10 March 1982, the Solar System briefly lined up for a cosmic family portrait: all nine then-recognized planets, from Mercury to Pluto, clustered on the same side of the Sun in a loose syzygy. Far from the doomsday hype it inspired, the alignment was gentle—but it gave astronomers a rare chance to study the gravitational choreography of our planetary neighborhood.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A planetary alignment fits perfectly with an episode built around shifting perspective on space and seeing emptiness as creative potential.

published.fact_text: When NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter slipped into Martian orbit on 10 March 2006, it became one of the most powerful cameras ever sent to another world. Its sharp-eyed instruments can spot objects as small as a desk on the surface, letting scientists watch avalanches, dust devils, and even the tracks of rovers creeping across the red planet.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A spacecraft called Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter pairs neatly with an episode whose journey begins with 'Solar Wind' and drifts through cosmic, exploratory soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On 10 March 1977, astronomers watching Uranus pass in front of a distant star noticed the starlight flicker before and after the planet's disk. Those tiny dips revealed a surprise: Uranus is encircled by a system of dark, narrow rings, making it only the second planet after Saturn known to wear a delicate band of dust and ice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: Newly discovered planetary rings echo the looping, orbital feel of 'Cosmic Monday' and its spacey disco lineage.

published.fact_text: On 10 March 1535, Bishop Tomás de Berlanga's ship drifted off course in windless seas, delivering him by accident to the volcanic shores of the Galápagos Islands. His letters describing giant tortoises and tame seabirds became some of the earliest European records of a place that would later reshape biology through Darwin's theory of evolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: An accidental voyage to a wildlife-rich archipelago mirrors the episode’s meditative journey into remote waters and charismatic animals.

published.fact_text: On 10 March 1496, after founding the city of Santo Domingo in the Caribbean, Christopher Columbus departed for Spain, leaving his brother in charge of Europe's first permanent settlement in the Americas. The city would become a laboratory of cultural collision, where European, Indigenous, and African histories intertwined in ways that still echo today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A seafaring expedition that seeds a new city pairs with an episode literally recorded at sea, centered on voyages and shared journeys.

published.fact_text: On 10 March 1876, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful telephone call, summoning his assistant with the words, “Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you.” That crackling sentence, transmitted over a wire in Boston, opened an era in which human voices could leap across cities and, eventually, around the world.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The birth of long-distance voice communication aligns with Sedna’s first broadcast, both marking the beginning of new ways to send voices into the world.
